claim_text: Although HFEN correlates remarkably strongly with DFDs and reader scores, our reader study uses single-coil reconstructions whereas the dar2019-style synthesized MR images are typically multi-coil. Whether HFEN's strong correlation transfers to multi-coil settings is an open question.

sub_claims.text: dar2019 is a representative MR image synthesis / cross-modality (cross-contrast) work.

evidence.snippet: Image Synthesis in Multi-Contrast MRI with Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks

Here we propose a new approach for multi-contrast MRI synthesis based on conditional generative adversarial networks. ... Demonstrations on T1- and T2-weighted images from healthy subjects and patients clearly indicate the superior performance of the proposed approach compared to previous state-of-the-art methods.

Here we propose a novel approach for image synthesis in multi-contrast MRI based on generative adversarial network (GAN) architectures. ... our proposed method employs conditional GANs to synthesize images in the target contrast given input images in the source contrast.

Cross-domain synthesis of medical images have recently been gaining popularity in medical imaging. Given a subject's image b1 in M1 (source domain), the aim is to accurately estimate the respective image of the same subject b2 in M2 (target domain).

A multi-contrast MRI synthesis approach based on conditional GANs was demonstrated against state-of-the-art methods in three publicly available brain MRI datasets (MIDAS, IXI, BRATS). ... While our synthesis approach was primarily demonstrated for multi-contrast brain MRI here, it may offer similar performance benefits in synthesis across imaging modalities such as MRI, CT or PET.

sub_claims.nuance: dar2019 is explicitly a multi-contrast MR image synthesis paper using conditional GANs (pGAN / cGAN); the paper itself frames its method as applicable to cross-modality synthesis (MRI, CT, PET), squarely placing it in the cross-modality / MR synthesis category.

sub_claims.text: The MR images used/synthesized in dar2019 are multi-coil (as opposed to single-coil).

evidence.snippet: The experiments were performed on three separate datasets: the MIDAS dataset, the IXI dataset and the BRATS dataset. MIDAS and IXI datasets primarily contained data from healthy subjects, whereas the BRATS datasets contained data from patients with structural abnormality.

T1-weighted images: 3D gradient-echo sequence, TR=14ms, TE=7.7ms, flip angle=25, matrix size=256x176, 1 mm isotropic resolution, axial orientation. T2-weighted images: 2D spin-echo sequence, TR=7730ms, TE=80ms, flip angle=180, matrix size=256x192, 1 mm isotropic resolution, axial orientation.

T1-weighted images: TR=9.813ms, TE=4.603ms, flip angle=80, volume size = 256 x 256 x 150, voxel dimensions = 0.94 x 0.94 x 1.2 mm3, sagittal orientation. T2-weighted images: TR=8178ms, TE=100ms, flip angle=90, volume size = 256 x 256 x 150, voxel dimensions = 0.94 x 0.94 x 1.2 mm3, axial orientation.

The implementation of the pGAN network provided in https://github.com/junyanz/pytorch-CycleGAN-and-pix2pix was used based on 256x256 images. Thus all MR images were zero-padded in the image-domain to this size prior to training.

sub_claims.nuance: dar2019 never discusses coils, coil-combination, parallel imaging, SENSE/GRAPPA/ESPIRiT, or k-space. It operates entirely on pre-reconstructed, intensity-normalized 2D brain MR images from three public datasets (MIDAS, IXI, BRATS). Whether the underlying acquisitions were multi-coil is standard for clinical MRI but is not a property the paper asserts or depends on, and the synthesis pipeline is image-domain, not coil-aware. The manuscript's framing that dar2019-style synthesized MR images are 'typically multi-coil' is an external inference not grounded in this paper.

remediation.suggested_edit: Consider softening or rescoping: e.g., 'whereas the dar2019 setting uses image-domain multi-contrast brain MR data (for which the underlying acquisitions are typically multi-coil in clinical practice).' dar2019 itself does not characterize its inputs as multi-coil; it uses publicly available pre-reconstructed image-domain datasets (MIDAS/IXI/BRATS) and does not discuss coil combination or parallel imaging.

phrasings_tried: Is dar2019 an MR image synthesis / cross-modality paper?
Does dar2019 describe its MR images as multi-coil, and does it involve coil-combination, parallel imaging, SENSE/GRAPPA/ESPIRiT, or k-space processing?
What datasets does dar2019 use and at what stage (image-domain vs k-space / coil-domain) does it operate?
Does dar2019 synthesize images that are inherently multi-coil, or does it synthesize already-reconstructed magnitude images?
attestation.closest_adjacent: Closest adjacent passage to any coil/multi-coil discussion: the dataset descriptions (MIDAS/IXI/BRATS) list sequence parameters (TR, TE, flip angle, voxel size, orientation) but no coil/channel information. The network-training description specifies 2D 256x256 image-domain inputs, confirming image-domain (not coil-domain) processing. No mention of 'coil', 'multi-coil', 'single-coil', 'SENSE', 'GRAPPA', 'ESPIRiT', 'parallel imaging', or 'k-space' appears anywhere in the paper content.
attestation.indirect_attribution_check: dar2019 does not credit another primary for the claim that its synthesized images are multi-coil; it simply does not make such a claim. The multi-coil framing originates from the manuscript's discussion of HFEN generalization, not from dar2019.
attestation.out_of_context_check: The manuscript uses dar2019 as an exemplar of the 'synthesized MR image' setting to argue that such settings are 'typically multi-coil.' dar2019 itself is ambivalent on this: it operates in the image domain on public brain MRI datasets whose coil structure is not discussed. Thus the 'typically multi-coil' attribute is the manuscript author's generalization about MR synthesis, not a property established by dar2019.
